# TCP vs UDP

## Overview

**TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** are the two core transport layer protocols in the Internet protocol suite. They serve different purposes depending on whether reliability or speed is the priority.

---

## Comparison Table

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Transmission Control Protocol | User Datagram Protocol |
| **Connection Type** | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| **Handshake** | 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) | None |
| **Reliability** | Guaranteed delivery | No guarantee |
| **Ordering** | Packets arrive in order | No ordering guarantee |
| **Error Checking** | Yes — retransmits lost packets | Basic checksum only |
| **Flow Control** | Yes | No |
| **Congestion Control** | Yes | No |
| **Speed** | Slower (more overhead) | Faster (less overhead) |
| **Header Size** | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| **Data Boundaries** | Stream-based (no boundaries) | Message-based (discrete packets) |
| **Broadcast Support** | No | Yes |
| **Use Case** | Accuracy-critical apps | Speed-critical apps |

---

## When to Use Each

### ✅ Use TCP when:
- Data **must arrive completely and correctly**
- Order of data matters
- Examples:
  - Web browsing (HTTP/HTTPS)
  - Email (SMTP, IMAP)
  - File transfers (FTP, SFTP)
  - Database queries

### ✅ Use UDP when:
- **Speed matters more** than perfect delivery
- Some data loss is acceptable
- Examples:
  - Video/audio streaming (YouTube, Netflix)
  - Online gaming
  - Live broadcasts
  - DNS lookups
  - VoIP (Zoom, Skype)

---

## How TCP's 3-Way Handshake Works

```
Client          Server
  |                |
  |--- SYN
